introduction: An infectious protozoan disease transmitted by the female Anopheles mosquito. A major public and private health problem and indeed a cause and consequence of national underdevelopment. Five species of the parasite cause the disease in humans: Plasmodium falciparum, P. malariae, P. vivax, P. ovale and P. knowlesi . P. falciparum accounts for 98% of all cases of malaria in Nigeria and is responsible for the severe form of the disease. Principal mode of spread; bites from infected female Anopheles mosquito. Peak feeding times are usually dusk and dawn, but also throughout the night. Other uncommon modes are: Blood transfusion, Mother-to-child transmission.

features: Multiple convulsions - more than 2 episodes in 24 hours

clinical_features.type: Indicators of poor prognosis in severe malaria

features: Blood film showing >20% of parasite to be 'late stage'
Blood film showing > 5% of neutrophils with visible pigments

investigations: Stool microscopy for ova; occult blood
Cerebrospinal fluid biochemistry; microscopy, culture, and sensitivity

goals: Attend to the immediate threats of life.
drug: Prompt parasitological confirmation by microscopy or RDTs before treatment
Artemether-Lumefantrine (AL) is the medicine of choice
Artesunate-Amodiaquine (AA), Dihydroartemisinin Piperaquine and Artesunate-Pyronaridine are alternatives
Pre-referral treatment (Rectal Artesunate, Artesunate IM, Artemether IM or Quinine IM)
Parenteral Artesunate is the drug of choice for severe malaria
Follow-on Treatment with a full course of ACT
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Mefloquine should be avoided if the patient had cerebral malaria because of the increased risk of seizure, encephalopathy and psychosis.
supportive_measures: If temperature is >38.5°C, wipe with wet towel, and fan to lower the temperature
Nurse in cardiac position for Pulmonary oedema
Renal failure - Give fluids if patient is dehydrated
Transfuse with screened fresh whole blood for profuse bleeding.
If meningitis is suspected, give appropriate antibiotics.

prevention: Reduce the frequency of mosquito bites by avoiding exposure to mosquitoes at their peak feeding times.
Chemoprohylaxis is indicated for children born to non-immune mothers in endemic areas, pregnant women, travellers to endemic areas, and people with sickle cell anaemia.
Mefloquine and Atovaquone-Proguanil are recommended for use in Nigeria.
Chemoprophylaxis is not recommended for individuals living with areas of intense transmission.
